items.rationale: Believes NFTs bootstrap communities, confer 'psychological ownership,' and let brands share value with users — turning consumers into builders. His central, most-restated thesis; he's writing a book on it.
items.age_note: Consistent and continually restated from Jan 2022 through June 2023; most recent statement is current.

citations.quote: Some NFTs just serve as ownership records, while others can become the foundation of digital brands!
Web3 platforms unlock a novel and especially powerful form of network effect through community engagement and social cohesion.

items.rationale: Sees cc0 as inviting derivative creation and co-creation that grows the value of the original asset; a 'literal license to build.' Explicitly notes it is not one-size-fits-all.
items.age_note: Core to Aug 2022 article and reaffirmed in April 2023 wave-mint piece; current.

citations.quote: cc0 licensing is an incredibly compelling way to kickstart IP that may one day take on a life of its own.
A 'wave mint' leverages people's on-chain data and reputation to select for holders who are well matched to a project's culture

items.rationale: His own economic-theory result: public on-chain transaction history lets entrants poach rivals' loyal customers, restoring competition and driving prices down — net good for consumers.
items.age_note: Most recent and actively-promoted research thread (March–May 2023); current.

citations.quote: vampire attacks can result in virtuous platform competition that drives down prices.
Blood-sucking web platforms may be good for consumers.

items.rationale: Argues the 2022 CeFi blowups vindicate decentralized finance rather than condemning crypto; decentralization reduces counterparty/custody risk.

citations.quote: Do the collapses of FTX, Celsius, and Terra signal the end of crypto? No, but they do show why we need #DeFi.

items.rationale: Strong market-design preference for clearing-price auctions and reputation-gated 'wave' mints over descending pay-as-bid; sees good mechanism design as solving supply/demand and bot/gas problems.
items.age_note: Persistent from early 2022; reaffirmed June 2023 — fully current.

citations.quote: I generally prefer clearing-price auctions to descending pay-as-bid auctions
SO BULLISH on @jenkinsthevalet @TallyLabsNFT running an actual clearing-price auction on-chain, rather than the descending pay-as-bid auctions

items.rationale: Argues 'location, location, location' applies to scarce, non-fungible metaverse spaces; interoperability/portability across worlds raises value. Enthusiastic about generative architecture (SOLIDS) as portable building primitives.
items.age_note: Heaviest output mid-2022; lighter but still referenced into 2023 (advisor relationships ongoing). Slight cooling in emphasis vs. NFT-brand thesis.

citations.quote: It matters which metaverse location you own! (The spaces themselves are non-fungible in that sense.)
My friend @0xfar has just launched something really cool: a generative architecture project called @SOLIDSNFT

items.rationale: Frames decentralization as adjustable 'slider bars' per business unit; centralize early to find product-market fit, then decentralize — design for it upfront as competitive advantage.

citations.quote: Decentralization needn't be 'all-or-nothing.' But you have to plan in advance
the one that's better set up for it upfront is likely to be the winner.

items.rationale: Worries the prevalent descending pay-as-bid format and demand/supply imbalance produce gas wars and overpayment; a botched auction can deter projects from better formats. Repeatedly flags poorly-designed mints (Azuki Elementals, GutterCatGang).
items.age_note: Recurring 2022–2023; sharpest in June 2023 Azuki critique — current.

citations.quote: And fundamentally gas wars are about supply/demand (im)balance
this fiasco will probably deter future projects from trying it.

items.rationale: Views centralized crypto platforms taking on excess/opaque risk as the failure mode behind FTX/Celsius/Terra; competition only helps if firms don't engage in such malbehavior.

citations.quote: such as with centralized crypto finance platforms taking on excess risk

items.rationale: Pushes back hard on framing NFTs as a sure way to make money; stresses they are inherently risky at any price; recurrently warns about misleading press, scams, and rugpulls.

citations.quote: BUYING NFTS AS INVESTMENTS IS INHERENTLY RISKY. At any price point. No investment is guaranteed to go up!

items.rationale: Even while excited about vampire attacks, he flags that current implementations may not be durable and lean on regulatorily questionable incentive strategies.
items.age_note: Caveat stated alongside the March 2023 thesis; current.

citations.quote: it's not clear how sustainable the current vampire attack models are, especially since many of them rely on strategies that are regulatorily suspect.

items.rationale: Repeatedly worried about scam ads, fake/compromised accounts, and the security risk of minting from wallets holding high-value assets; treats this as a real adoption obstacle.

citations.quote: a wave mint is complex to execute. And there are security issues around having people mint for wallets
Twitter for some reason is curreny allowing what appear to be scam ads from a fake copy

items.rationale: Treats Ethereum as foundational infrastructure for NFTs and web3 rather than a price bet; celebrated The Merge as a landmark engineering feat. Enthusiasm is about the platform, not a directional price call.
items.age_note: Merge enthusiasm Sept 2022; no later price view. Infrastructure framing, not a trading thesis.

citations.quote: GM! Happy Merge Day to all who celebrate, QED
it's like changing the engine of a plane while it's in the air... but at the scale of the Internet

items.rationale: Cites Polygon favorably as a cheap, practical settlement layer for putting verifiable records on-chain — a utility/infrastructure endorsement, not an investment view.

citations.quote: Why would it be expensive? You could do it on polygon for pennies.

risk_regime.rationale: Through the 2022–2023 bear market and the FTX/Celsius/Terra collapses, Kominers stayed structurally and behaviorally bullish on crypto/web3: he joined a16z crypto research, kept advising projects, launched products (wave mint), is writing an NFT book, and explicitly rejected the 'end of crypto' narrative. His optimism is long-horizon and builder-oriented, not a tactical price call — and is tempered by genuine concern about CeFi risk-taking, scams, and bad mechanism design.

notes: Kominers is an NFT / market-design / web3-strategy specialist, NOT a fungible-token picker — so the signal is overwhelmingly in sectors, not tokens. tokens_excited entries (ETH, Polygon) are infrastructure/utility endorsements, not directional price views; conviction is scaled down accordingly. tokens_concerned is empty because he never expresses forward-looking concern about a specific token (Terra/Celsius/FTX appear only as retrospective evidence for the CeFi-risk sector view, not as token calls). Recency weighting applied: the NFT-brand, vampire-attack/platform-competition, and NFT-auction-mechanism theses are his most current and most-restated (through June 2023); metaverse/digital-land enthusiasm is downweighted slightly as its emphasis peaked mid-2022 though it never reversed. His huge volume of pandemic-policy, pure-math, and Wordle content was excluded as off-topic to market views. Most positions are 'grounded' with current citations; none of his crypto stances show a clear reversal over the corpus window — concerns and excitements coexist (e.g., bullish on vampire attacks while flagging their regulatory fragility).
